stories.title: Union Investment, Nuveen Sell Upper East Side Retail
stories.excerpt: Union Investment and Nuveen Real Estate have sold their 1511 Third Ave. retail property on Manhattan’s Upper East Side to San Francisco-based Stockbridge. Terms of the sale were not disclosed. Union Investment acquired the retail property in 2016 as part of a closed joint venture deal with Nuveen Real Estate (then TH Real Estate) for its open-ended real estate fund Unilmmo: Global, which held a 49% stake. The fully leased property spans approximately 17,500 square feet over four floors plus a basement. The main portion of the ground floor and basement is leased Gap. The upper floors are occupied by luxury fitness company Equinox. “In a transaction market still characterized by limited transaction activity and high U.S. interest rates, we took the opportunity to sell the retail property,” said Kseniya Merritt, SVP and head of retail investments North America at Union Investment. “The exit is being made for strategic portfolio reasons and improves the fund’s liquidity position.” The CBRE New York retail team, lead by Doug Middleton and Jack Stillwagon, brokered the sale. The post Union Investment, Nuveen Sell Upper East Side Retail appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Union Investment, Nuveen exit Upper East Side retail to Stockbridge; 17,500 SF fully leased.
Sellers cited limited transaction activity, high U.S. rates, and portfolio liquidity needs.
Manhattan retail market remains constrained; limited deal flow persists despite stabilized tenancy.
stories.summary: Union Investment and Nuveen Real Estate sold their fully leased 17,500-square-foot retail property at 1511 Third Ave. on Manhattan's Upper East Side to San Francisco-based Stockbridge. The sellers cited the challenging transaction environment—marked by limited activity and elevated interest rates—as rationale for the exit, which was framed as improving fund liquidity. The property's stable occupancy (Gap on ground/basement, Equinox on upper floors) did not offset sellers' preference to reduce exposure in a constrained capital market.
stories.impact: A portfolio exit by large institutional holders in Manhattan retail signals continued pressure on capital formation for real estate. For multifamily developers, this underscores the broader tightening of credit availability and rising cost of capital affecting all property types in NYC, making acquisition and development financing more expensive and selective.

stories.title: Inside litigation surrounding Amrit Ocean Resort condo-hotel
stories.excerpt: In December 2023, David Stern closed on his $1.8 million purchase of a unit at Amrit Ocean Resort, an oceanfront condo-hotel on Singer Island, a barrier island north of Palm Beach.  The following year, he found himself in the courts, suing the developer and managers of the LLCs that built the two-tower project on a nearly 7-acre plot of land at 3100 North Ocean Drive in Riviera Beach. His lawsuit hinged on the Florida statute governing condo associations and turnover of the associations’ boards. Stern alleged the developer failed to hand over control to the unit owners in a timely […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Amrit Ocean Resort (Riviera Beach, FL) faced owner litigation over construction quality, governance.
Unit owner purchased Dec. 2023 at $1.8M; dozens of owners reported unit/amenity defects.
Settled lawsuit reflects rising condo construction litigation in South Florida; favorable developer laws.
stories.summary: David Stern and other unit owners at the oceanfront Amrit Ocean Resort condo-hotel in Riviera Beach, Florida sued the developer over shoddy construction, broken promises, and delayed board turnover. Stern's case was settled after being scheduled for trial; the suit was one of several against the developer since the project's completion. The property faced a broader pattern of owner complaints regarding unit conditions and amenities, symptomatic of rising condo-construction litigation across South Florida as delayed projects reach completion.
stories.impact: Construction quality disputes and litigation delays at completed condo-hotels in South Florida signal execution and legal risk for mixed-use residential developers in the region. Multifamily developers should monitor labor, contractor, and materials quality control tightly, particularly in South Florida, where owner litigation and governance disputes are becoming more frequent and costly as projects deliver.

stories.title: ANALYSIS: Warsh’s Fed Sends Blunt Message
stories.excerpt: Kevin Warsh has chaired his first Federal Open Market Committee meeting, and he wasted no time making clear this is a different central bank. The FOMC held the federal funds rate at 3.50%–3.75% on Wednesday, the fourth consecutive meeting without a move. The decision was unanimous and widely expected. What wasn’t fully priced in was the degree to which the accompanying statement, and the person now running the institution, would signal a clean break from the posture of the Jerome Powell era.  A Shorter Statement The policy statement was notably terser than anything produced under Powell. Gone was the language committing to the Fed’s 2% inflation goal. In its place: “the committee will deliver price stability.” Gone too was the so-called easing bias — the conditional language that had outlined the circumstances under which the Fed might consider cutting rates. No replacement language was offered.  Warsh has long been skeptical of forward guidance, and he backed that up immediately by declining to submit his own interest rate forecast to the quarterly dot plot — an unusual move that itself sent a signal about how he intends to lead.  The Dot Plot Flip In March, 12 of 19 officials expected at least one rate cut this year, and no one anticipated hikes. Wednesday’s dot plot looked almost nothing like that. Nine officials now expect at least one quarter-point increase by year-end. Eight see rates unchanged. Only one still pencils in a cut. The median projection now points to a hike.  The Fed’s Summary of Economic Projections shows no rate reduction expected in 2026, with the policy rate now seen ending the year at 3.8%, up from the March projection of 3.4%. The rate path beyond that also shifted higher: 3.6% in 2027 and 3.4% in 2028, compared to prior estimates of 3.1% for both years. The longer-run rate holds at 3.1%.  Two-Sided Paralysis Mar
bullets: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh held rates at 3.50%–3.75%; removed forward guidance on cuts.
Dot plot now shows nine officials expecting rate hikes; one expecting cuts; median projects hike.
2026 federal funds rate raised to 3.8% (from 3.4% in March); 'higher for longer' confirmed.
stories.summary: Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh presided over his first FOMC meeting, holding rates steady but signaling a hawkish pivot away from the Powell era. The central bank removed forward guidance suggesting rate cuts, and the dot plot flipped dramatically—nine officials now expect rate hikes by year-end, only one expects a cut, and the median 2026 projection now targets 3.8% rather than 3.4%. Warsh declined to submit his own rate forecast, underlining the Fed's shift away from predictability and toward a "higher for longer" stance.
stories.impact: A materially hawkish Fed pivot directly raises the cost of capital for multifamily development and acquisition across all markets. Higher-for-longer interest rates increase debt service on construction loans and refinances, compress cap rates on property valuations, and lengthen underwriting timeframes—constraining starts and forcing developers to improve yields or reduce leverage, favoring larger, well-capitalized firms.

stories.title: Browning Real Estate Partners to Break Ground on First Ohio Industrial Development
stories.excerpt: Browning Real Estate Partners will break ground on a new 200,200-square-foot speculative industrial development in Canal Winchester, marking the company’s first ground-up development project in Ohio. The industrial facility will pursue LEED Silver certification and is being developed on approximately 16.33 acres within a planned industrial district. The project will operate under Browning’s Velocity industrial park brand, a portfolio of logistics developments across the Midwest supporting major national and regional occupiers. Designed to accommodate manufacturing, logistics, warehousing, and distribution users, the facility will feature 1,500 square feet of speculative office space. NAI Ohio Equities will oversee leasing for the project, with Curt Berlin, Matthew Osowski, Blake DeCrane, and Kaydee Hurst serving as exclusive leasing agents. “Browning continues to be one of the most respected industrial developers in the Midwest, and we are excited to partner with them on the leasing of Velocity Hill Road,” said Berlin. The post Browning Real Estate Partners to Break Ground on First Ohio Industrial Development appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Browning Real Estate breaks ground on 200,200 SF Velocity industrial park in Canal Winchester, OH.
First Ohio ground-up for the Midwest developer; LEED Silver spec facility on 16.33 acres.
NAI Ohio Equities leasing; designed for manufacturing, logistics, warehousing, distribution tenants.
stories.summary: Browning Real Estate Partners is breaking ground on its first Ohio development—a 200,200-square-foot speculative industrial facility in Canal Winchester's planned industrial district. The project, branded Velocity Hill Road, sits on 16.33 acres, pursues LEED Silver certification, and includes 1,500 square feet of speculative office. NAI Ohio Equities is handling leasing; the facility targets manufacturing, logistics, warehousing, and distribution operators.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact to multifamily. However, industrial expansion into Ohio (particularly near Columbus) signals confidence in Midwest logistics demand and regional supply-chain resilience, which may attract population and job growth that eventually benefits multifamily demand in secondary and tertiary Ohio markets.

stories.title: Hawkins Way Capital Drops $28M on 81 East Third Street Apartments, Student Housing
stories.excerpt: Beverly Hills-based real estate investment company Hawkins Way Capital has acquired a property comprising student housing and market-rate apartments at 81 East Third Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The $28 million acquisition in the East Village closed Wednesday, according to a source familiar with the deal. The sale comes just days after CO reported that Hawkins purchased the Upper West Side residence hall of AMDA College of the Performing Arts at 117 West 70th Street for $80 million. The 13-story building at 81 East Third Street, between First and Second avenues, spans 36,047 square feet and contains 45 residential units, according to Department of Finance records. Roughly two-thirds of the building is occupied by students at the New York Conservatory for Dramatic Arts, which uses the site as a dormitory. A school brochure for the property advertises suite-style dorms with a laundry room and common areas. The student housing portion of the building will remain intact, according to a source, but the fate of the apartments is unclear. Hawkins did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The seller entity, 81 East 3 Street Realty, is linked to the Lokshin family, a local developer and landlord, as well as landlord Simon Fouladian. The entity redeveloped the building into student and rental housing in 2005, drawing the ire of its East Village neighbors over the building’s height. BKREA‘s Bob Knakal and Ana Barrie represented both sides of the sale. Spokespeople for BKREA and the sellers could not be reached for comment. Hawkins Way, which reports $3 billion in assets under management, has undertaken a series of student housing deals over the base year. In August 2025, the firm sold a former DoubleTree by Hilton hotel at 569 Lexington Avenue to the City University of New York for $125.6 million. The month before that, it acquired the 492-room student housing tower at 99 Washington Street in the Financial District. The firm also owns a 122-
bullets: Hawkins Way Capital acquires 81 East Third Street (East Village) for $28M; 45 units.
Building contains student housing (2/3) via NY Conservatory for Dramatic Arts; market-rate units (1/3).
Hawkins also bought AMDA College residence hall (Upper West Side) for $80M days prior.
stories.summary: Beverly Hills-based Hawkins Way Capital closed a $28 million acquisition of 81 East Third Street in Manhattan's East Village, a 13-story, 36,047-square-foot building holding 45 residential units. Roughly two-thirds of the building operates as a dormitory for the New York Conservatory for Dramatic Arts; the remainder is market-rate apartments. The deal follows Hawkins' $80 million purchase of the AMDA College residence hall on the Upper West Side just days earlier, signaling a strategic focus on student-housing-anchored assets in Manhattan.
stories.impact: Hawkins' dual-acquisition strategy—targeting stabilized student-housing assets with blended market-rate components—exploits market inefficiency: student housing offers institutional tenant stability and counter-cyclical demand, while embedded market-rate units provide upside. For competing multifamily developers, this signals rising institutional capital focus on niche residential assets in Manhattan; competitive pricing and deal sourcing may tighten.

stories.title: Kadima Industrial Acquires Shallow Bay Woburn Property
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets arranged the sale and financing of 215 Salem St., a 62,155-square-foot shallow bay industrial facility in Woburn, MA. Director Tommy Hovey, senior director David Coffman and managing director Michael Restivo represented the seller in the sale to Kadima Industrial Partners. Working on behalf of the new owner, director Ryan Parker on JLL’s Debt Advisory team arranged the acquisition financing with Webster 5 Cent Savings Bank. Built in 1984, the property is positioned in Greater Boston’s Route 128 North submarket, 11 miles north of downtown Boston. The facility benefits from direct access to Interstates 93 and 95 and Route 3, providing connectivity to the region’s primary logistics networks and employment centers. “The combination of last-mile access to Boston, multiple highway connections and a diverse tenant roster spanning logistics, manufacturing and service sectors made this an exceptionally compelling acquisition for Kadima,” Hovey said. “Properties of this size and configuration in established infill locations continue to attract substantial buyer interest as land constraints limit new development.” The post Kadima Industrial Acquires Shallow Bay Woburn Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kadima Industrial acquires 215 Salem St., Woburn MA; 62,155 SF shallow-bay industrial, built 1984.
Route 128 North submarket, 11 miles from Boston; direct I-93/I-95/Route 3 access.
Diverse tenant base (logistics, manufacturing, services); JLL arranged sale and Webster 5 financing.
stories.summary: Kadima Industrial Partners acquired 215 Salem Street, a 62,155-square-foot shallow-bay industrial property in Woburn, Massachusetts, via JLL's arrangement of sale and Webster 5 Cent Savings Bank financing. Built in 1984 and positioned in the Route 128 North submarket (11 miles north of Boston), the property benefits from direct highway access and a diversified tenant roster spanning logistics, manufacturing, and services. JLL noted that properties of this size and configuration in established infill locations attract substantial buyer interest as land constraints limit new development.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, sustained strong demand for infill industrial assets in Greater Boston reflects robust regional logistics and e-commerce demand, signaling job and population retention in the metro. This supports long-term rental demand fundamentals for multifamily in Boston-area markets, particularly inner-ring suburbs accessible to I-93 and I-95 corridors.

stories.title: Warsh’s First Fed Meeting as Chair Produces a Result Many in CRE Expected
stories.excerpt: President Donald Trump nominated Kevin Warsh as Federal Reserve chairman in the hope of lowering interest rates, but the first Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting under his leadership signaled a continued higher-for-longer environment. The central bank, in a unanimous 12-0 vote Wednesday, kept its benchmark interest rate at between 3.5 percent and 3.75 percent for the fourth straight meeting, citing inflation uncertainty tied to the war in Iran. The Fed also released an updated quarterly “dot plot” matrix of individual committees that removed prior forecasts of one rate cut by the end of this year and signaled possible rate hikes. The updated, more hawkish FOMC projections, which Warsh opted to not participate in, is now showing a federal funds rate of 3.8 percent at the end of 2026, up from 3.4 percent estimated in March, with nine of the 18 members signaling a near-term hike. “Economic activity is expanding at a solid pace despite elevated uncertainty that owes in part to conflict in the Middle East,” Warsh said in prepared remarks to open his post-meeting press conference. “Productivity growth and capital investment are both strong, job gains have kept pace with the workforce, and the unemployment rate has changed little.” The unanimous Fed decision Wednesday marked a stark contrast to an 8-4 vote on April 29 to maintain interest rates, a vote that marked the highest level of dissent since October 1992. The FOMC board includes former Fed chair Jerome Powell, who opted to remain as a governor and is eligible to stay in the post through January 2028. Warsh said he is forming five task forces that will determine the Fed’s use and reliance on existing data and the central bank’s inflation framework. He also announced a preference to remove “forward guidance” formerly used by the FOMC to shape future monetary policy decisions. Jay Neveloff, partner and chair of U.S. real estate at HSF Kramer, said Warsh may alter some of the ways the Fed examines data s
bullets: Kevin Warsh Fed keeps rates at 3.5%–3.75%; cites inflation, Middle East conflict uncertainty.
Updated dot plot removes 2025 rate-cut forecasts; signals possible hikes; 3.8% end-2026 rate.
Warsh declined to submit rate forecast; signals Fed will resist forward guidance and market expectations.
stories.summary: Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh's first FOMC meeting produced a unanimous 12-0 vote to hold rates at 3.5%–3.75% and a notably hawkish policy shift. The updated dot plot eliminated prior expectations of rate cuts by year-end 2025 and now projects a 3.8% federal funds rate at end-2026, with nine of 18 officials signaling near-term hikes. Warsh's refusal to submit his own rate forecast signals the Fed will not offer the forward guidance markets had anticipated under Powell, undercutting expectations for near-term relief.
stories.impact: A higher-for-longer, less predictable Fed stance directly raises development and acquisition costs across multifamily. Developers face extended holding periods on underwritten returns, tighter debt availability, compressed valuations, and increased refinance risk—pressuring project feasibility and returns, particularly for lower-yielding stabilized assets.

stories.title: Industrial Property Near Indianapolis Airport Changes Hands
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap completed the sale of 7770 W. New York St., a 16,000-square-foot single-tenant industrial property in Indianapolis. Located near the Indianapolis International Airport, the property sold for $2.1 million to Greenspring Realty Partners. “This transaction highlights the continued strength of Indianapolis’ industrial market, particularly in this highly desirable airport-area submarket,” said Gus Poulos, associate director of investments. Built in 1994, the property spans 2.20 acres and is occupied by Terracon Consultants, an employee-owned firm that provides environmental consulting, geotechnical engineering, construction materials testing and facility engineering services. “We were able to bring the seller and out-of-state buyer together and closed the deal in cash just 29 days after signing our representation agreement,” said Bender, managing director investments. “The product type and market speak for themselves when it comes to Indianapolis industrial real estate.” Poulos and Forest Bender, investment specialists in Marcus & Millichap’s Indianapolis office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the private in-state seller. The post Industrial Property Near Indianapolis Airport Changes Hands appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap sells 7770 W. New York St., Indianapolis (16,000 SF industrial) for $2.1M.
Greenspring Realty Partners buyer; near Indianapolis International Airport; occupied by Terracon.
Single-tenant, 1994-built property; 2.20 acres; quick close (29 days) signals market strength.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap completed the sale of 7770 West New York Street, a 16,000-square-foot single-tenant industrial property near Indianapolis International Airport, for $2.1 million to Greenspring Realty Partners. Built in 1994 and leased to Terracon Consultants, the property spans 2.20 acres. The deal closed in 29 days on a cash basis, exemplifying strong demand for airport-proximate industrial assets in Indianapolis.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, sustained strong transaction velocity in Indianapolis industrial, particularly near the airport, reflects robust last-mile logistics demand and supply-chain confidence, supporting job retention and population stability in the metro—positive underpinnings for multifamily demand in Indianapolis.

stories.title: Kushner, Cain pay $43M for Edgewater site amid apartment rent-growth predictions
stories.excerpt: Cain and Kushner paid $43.1 million for a site in Edgewater where they’ve been planning a 364-unit luxury apartment tower.  The firms bought the 1.5-acre property at 614 and 720 Northeast 27th Street from an entity tied to a joint venture between Miami-based OKO Group and Cain.  London-based Cain, led by Jonathan Goldstein, and New York-based Kushner, headed by Laurent Morali and Nicole Kushner Meyer, have been planning the 40-story project since April. BDT & MSD Partners provided a $42 million loan for the development, with construction expected to start late next year, The Real Deal previously reported.  The property […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Cain, Kushner acquire 1.5-acre Edgewater (Miami) site for $43.1M; 364-unit luxury tower planned.
BDT & MSD Partners provided $42M construction loan; 40-story building; construction late 2025.
South Florida apartment supply down 7.8% YoY; strong demand; modest rent growth predicted; ranked #2 investor market.
stories.summary: London-based Cain and New York-based Kushner paid $43.1 million for a 1.5-acre site in Miami's Edgewater, where they plan a 40-story, 364-unit luxury apartment tower. BDT & MSD Partners provided $42 million in development financing, with construction expected to launch in late 2025. The acquisition marks the first project under a new Cain-Kushner South Florida joint venture. South Florida's apartment supply sits 7.8% below the three-year average; demand remains strong as elevated homeownership costs sustain rental appetite, with modest rent growth expected and multifamily ranking second in institutional investor intentions for 2025.
stories.impact: Cain-Kushner's entry into the Edgewater market at $43M for 364 units (~$118K/unit acquisition cost) signals continued capital aggregation in South Florida's tight supply environment. Strong institutional co-investment, constrained new supply (7.8% below average), and persistent demand fundamentals create a favorable window for existing and pipeline multifamily assets in Miami, supporting pricing power and investor returns across South Florida markets.

stories.title: UBS offloads mostly vacant Downtown Houston office building ripe for conversion
stories.excerpt: UBS Realty Investors recently offloaded a mostly vacant Downtown Houston office building considered ripe for residential conversion.  The Swiss bank’s real estate arm sold the 24-story, 542,000-square-foot building at 919 Milam Street, deed records show. The buyer is Tampa-based apartment developer Zhukovskyi Development. Realty News Report first reported the trade.  The property, which was built in 1956 and last renovated in 2023, is less than 20 percent occupied. Tenants include the Coronado Club, a members-only dining club. Terms of the sale were not disclosed, but the property was last valued for tax purposes at $48.3 million, appraisal district records show.  […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: UBS Realty offloads 919 Milam Street, Downtown Houston; 24-story, 542,000 SF, <20% occupied.
Buyer: Zhukovskyi Development (Tampa-based apt. developer); property valued $48.3M for tax purposes.
November 2023 Aecom report ranked property #2 for conversion viability; large floor plates (22,500 SF) challenge conversion.
stories.summary: UBS Realty Investors sold the 24-story, 542,000-square-foot building at 919 Milam Street in Downtown Houston to Tampa-based apartment developer Zhukovskyi Development. The property, built in 1956 and last renovated in 2023, is less than 20% occupied and last valued at $48.3 million for tax purposes. An Aecom report from November 2023 ranked the property second for residential conversion potential, though its large floor plates (22,500 square feet) complicate conversion economics.
stories.impact: Office-to-residential conversion activity in Houston signals acknowledgment by institutional sellers that downtown office recovery is prolonged; distressed asset pricing and conversion plays attract developer capital. For multifamily operators, this creates both upside (new inventory) and competitive pressure; conversion entails lease-up risk, capital intensity, and extended hold periods, favoring strong balance sheets. The market shift implies sustained softness in Houston CBD office demand, supporting rental apartment demand but increasing construction risk and cost per unit in conversions.

stories.title: City Invests $1B for New Affordable Housing Preservation Program
stories.excerpt: The New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) has put into action what it calls the Supportive Preservation Program (SPP), designed to provide greater support for and increase the preservation of affordable housing across the city’s five boroughs, the HPD announced Tuesday. Supportive housing around the city will see an investment of $1 billion from HPD, with a portion of that funding specifically dedicated to the preservation of New York City’s supportive housing supply, The Real Deal reported. The program is meant to specifically support units that provide services to people in danger of homelessness and facing other complex housing issues. That translates to more than 30,000 units. “What we’re announcing here today is a commitment to supportive housing in New York City and the New Yorkers who rely on it,” Patrick Love, deputy commissioner of development at HPD, said on Tuesday announcing the news. “The supportive housing model is a model that works.” Projects eligible for the SPP must be existing supportive housing projects in New York City and must have existing social service contracts administered by the city, according to the announcement. Through the SPP, the HPD will be able to grant tax exemptions, provide below-market loans and modify existing loans. Details about the timeline of HPD’s investment are unclear. “Supportive housing plays a critical role in our city, providing stability and services to New Yorkers in need. I am proud to be launching the new Supportive Preservation Program to ensure that providers and residents are able to succeed for many years to come,” Leila Bozorg, deputy mayor for housing and planning, said in a statement. “This program will be an important part of our holistic approach to provide and protect the housing that New Yorkers rely on.” Since entering office, Mayor Zohran Mamdani has made it his administration’s mission to supply New York City with more affordable housing. In May, the mayor an
bullets: NYC HPD launches Supportive Preservation Program (SPP); $1B investment in affordable housing.
Funding dedicated to preservation of supportive housing; >30,000 units providing services to homeless-risk populations.
Program expands public subsidy footprint in affordable/supportive housing; reduces market-rate development incentive.
stories.summary: New York City's Department of Housing Preservation and Development announced the Supportive Preservation Program, a $1 billion investment dedicated to preserving and expanding affordable and supportive housing across the five boroughs. The program targets more than 30,000 units that provide services to people at risk of homelessness or facing complex housing issues. The funding commitment underscores the city's focus on public-sector-led preservation rather than market-rate new supply.
stories.impact: NYC's $1B supportive-housing preservation program signals municipal priorities skewing toward subsidized units and away from market-rate development incentives. For multifamily developers, this constrains zoning headroom and increases regulatory burden in exchange for affordability mandates. Developers should expect tighter land availability, longer entitlements, and reduced density bonuses unless projects include significant affordable components, pressuring pro-forma returns in Manhattan and outer boroughs alike.

stories.title: Terreno Buys Maryland Industrial Property for $77M
stories.excerpt: Terreno Realty, a publicly traded owner of almost 20 million square feet of industrial real estate across six major U.S. markets, has acquired 3100-3300 Hubbard Road, an industrial property in Landover, Md., for approximately $77.1 million. The property includes three buildings totaling 305,000 square feet across 24 acres. It provides 49 dock-high and nine grade-level loading positions, according to a company announcement, and parking for 417 cars. The property is currently 92 percent leased to nine tenants. The purchase was completed June 16. Terreno did not immediately disclose the name of the seller or if brokers were involved in the transaction. Just one day prior, Terreno announced the purchase of 5751 General Washington Drive, a property in Alexandria, Va., hosting a 50,000-square-foot building on 2.8 acres, for $13 million. That property provides eight dock-high and one grade-level loading positions and parking for 73 cars, according to a company release. The identity of the buyer and the brokers were also unclear. According to its website, Terrano has offices in New York City and Bellevue, Wash., and its headquarters seems to be in San Francisco. It transacts in the New York City-Northern New Jersey area, as well as in Los Angeles, Miami, the San Francisco Bay area, Seattle and Washington, D.C. The company’s portfolio as of March 31 consisted of 19.9 million square feet and 310 buildings across 46 improved land parcels. Miami is currently the company’s largest market by square footage, with 42 buildings spanning 4.82 million square feet. Terreno also has 69 buildings across 3.58 million square feet in the New York City-Northern New Jersey market, and 27 buildings across 2.12 million square feet in the Washington, D.C., area. Terreno did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Larry Getlen can be reached at lgetlen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Terreno Realty acquires Landover, MD industrial (3100-3300 Hubbard Rd) for ~$77.1M; 305,000 SF.
Three buildings on 24 acres; 92% leased to nine tenants; 49 dock-high, 9 grade-level positions.
Day prior: Terreno bought 5751 General Washington Drive, Alexandria, VA (50,000 SF) for $13M.
stories.summary: Terreno Realty, a publicly traded industrial REIT, acquired 3100-3300 Hubbard Road in Landover, Maryland for approximately $77.1 million. The property comprises three buildings totaling 305,000 square feet across 24 acres, currently 92% leased to nine tenants, with 49 dock-high and nine grade-level loading positions. One day prior, Terreno closed on 5751 General Washington Drive in Alexandria, Virginia (50,000 square feet) for $13 million, signaling aggressive deployment of capital into trophy industrial assets in the Mid-Atlantic.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, Terreno's rapid-fire acquisitions of high-occupancy, well-leased industrial properties in Maryland and Virginia reflect strong demand for last-mile logistics infrastructure in the Washington-Baltimore corridor. This suggests robust supply-chain demand, population stability, and job growth in the region, indirectly supporting long-term multifamily fundamentals in Northern Virginia and the Baltimore metro.

stories.title: Pearson Ranch flattens with Tri Pointe development agreement
stories.excerpt: Tri Pointe Homes won approval to develop most of Pearson Ranch near Austin, forecasting more horizontal development in a project once billed as a dense mixed-use community. Williamson County approved a development agreement on Tuesday that clears the way for the Incline Village, Nevada-based homebuilder to take up 95 acres in the 156-acre Pearson Ranch, the Austin Business Journal reported. Initial plans for Pearson Ranch, proposed by Austin-based Inspire Development in January 2022, described a $2 billion mixed-use development with a 48-acre office campus, two hotels, 200,000 square feet of retail, restaurant and community space, as well as 2,500 multifamily […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tri Pointe Homes approved to develop 95 of 156 acres at Pearson Ranch near Austin.
Original Inspire mixed-use vision (2.5K multifamily units, office, hotel, retail) largely replaced by single-family.
Initial plan: $2B mixed-use; Tri Pointe's preliminary maps show residential neighborhoods supplanting commercial.
stories.summary: Williamson County approved a development agreement allowing Tri Pointe Homes to develop 95 of 156 acres at Pearson Ranch near Austin. The shift marks a dramatic departure from Inspire Development's 2022 mixed-use master plan, which called for 2,500 multifamily units, a 48-acre office campus, two hotels, and 200,000 square feet of retail and community space. Preliminary maps filed with Austin show Tri Pointe's residential neighborhoods (presumably single-family) supplanting much of the planned commercial, with only The Eden (306-unit multifamily) beginning construction in 2022.
stories.impact: Tri Pointe's substitution of single-family density for mixed-use (particularly multifamily) at Pearson Ranch signals a flight to lower-risk, higher-absorption residential product in the Austin market. For apartment developers, this reduces available land for multifamily near major employers (Dell) and reshapes the competitive landscape, pushing multifamily opportunities further into infill or requiring denser, urban-format projects. The shift also reflects builder preference for single-family in a market with elevated inventory and margin pressure.

stories.title: Cashmere Brand Kujten Picks Acadia’s 92 Greene Street for Second U.S. Location
stories.excerpt: French cashmere brand Kujten has leased a second Manhattan location less than a year after opening its first U.S. flagship along Madison Avenue. The upcoming store at Acadia Realty Trust’s 92 Greene Street, a midblock address bordered by Spring and Broome streets, sits in the heart of SoHo’s bustling shopping corridor. Kujten signed a long-term, 2,567-square-foot lease, with 1,531 square feet on the ground floor and 1,036 square feet on the lower level, according to landlord broker Newmark. The retailer was represented by Eric Le Goff and Max Kreinces of Retail by MONA, along with David Sitt of Status Capital. Newmark’s Ariel Schuster, Michael Paster and Gabe Staab negotiated on behalf of Acadia. The exact length of the lease, which was signed June 1, was not disclosed. The asking rent was $1.65 million per year. “Kujten is exactly the kind of brand that defines what SoHo is becoming — authentic, design-driven, and globally recognized,” Schuster said in a statement. “Bringing them to 92 Greene Street is a testament to the continued strength of this corridor and highlights the trend of global brands wanting to work with quality owners like Acadia.” Kujten opened its three-level, 4,400-square-foot flagship store at 831 Madison Avenue in September of last year. Founded in Paris in 2012, the brand is known for its delicate products and premium pricing, and hosts a network of more than 50 locations across France and the rest of Europe. Kujten is operated under parent company BCS Group, also headquartered in Paris. Kujten’s SoHo storefront is expected to open in fall of this year. The storefront will neighbor other high-ticket fashion brands on Greene Street, including Givenchy, Jil Sander and ETRO. Spokespeople for Kujten, Acadia, Retail by MONA and Status Capital did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: French cashmere brand Kujten leases 2,567 SF at Acadia Realty's 92 Greene Street (SoHo, NYC).
Second U.S. location, <1 year after Madison Ave flagship; 1,531 SF ground floor, 1,036 SF lower level.
Asking rent $1.65M/year; Newmark brokered; reflects strong SoHo retail demand and tenant anchoring.
stories.summary: Luxury French cashmere brand Kujten signed a long-term lease for 2,567 square feet at Acadia Realty Trust's 92 Greene Street in SoHo, Manhattan, marking its second U.S. location less than one year after opening a flagship on Madison Avenue. The space spans 1,531 square feet on the ground floor and 1,036 on the lower level; asking rent was $1.65 million per year. Newmark brokered the transaction.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, luxury branded retail clustering in SoHo and rapid expansion by high-end international retailers reflect strong foot traffic, affluent local demographics, and consumer confidence in Manhattan's prime shopping corridors. This supports residential rental demand and pricing power for luxury multifamily in adjacent neighborhoods (East Village, Nolita, Lower East Side).

stories.title: Gotham Org files plans for next phase of East New York project
stories.excerpt: David Picket’s Gotham Organization is forging ahead with its multiphase affordable housing project in East New York. The firm filed applications for three buildings in the Brooklyn neighborhood that are part of its Urban Innovative Village complex, Crain’s reported. The three buildings that make up the second phase of the project will combine for 599 permanently affordable housing units, according to the Commercial Observer, and one of the trio will be for senior housing. At 12040 Flatlands Avenue, Gotham is planning a 14-story building with 224 rental units and 5,300 square feet of community space. At 12030 Flatlands Avenue, there […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gotham Organization files Phase 2 (East New York, Brooklyn); 599 permanently affordable units.
Three buildings: 224 units (14-story), 206 senior units (13-story), 169 units (12-story); $315M loan closed April 2025.
Urban Innovative Village on 10.5-acre church campus; phase 1B delivered 386 units; phase 1 construction underway.
stories.summary: David Pickett's Gotham Organization filed applications for Phase 2 of its Urban Innovative Village complex in East New York, comprising three buildings with a combined 599 permanently affordable units, including dedicated senior housing. The 224-unit, 14-story building at 12040 Flatlands Avenue will include 5,300 square feet of community space; the 13-story, 206-unit senior building at 12030 Flatlands Avenue will have 2,900 square feet of retail; and the 12-story, 169-unit building at 395 Louisiana Avenue will offer 5,200 square feet of retail. Gotham closed a $315 million construction loan in April 2025 for Phase 2, building on Phase 1B (386 units, $270M loan in 2024).
stories.impact: Gotham's large-scale affordable-housing initiative in East New York, anchored by institutional debt financing and community partnerships, demonstrates the viability of mission-driven multifamily development in outer-borough submarkets with public subsidy. For competing multifamily developers, this signals strong capital availability for affordable/supportive housing projects and validates demand in East Brooklyn, but it also consumes subsidy and financing capacity, potentially crowding out market-rate development in the area.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending June 12, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was in Brooklyn. The Gotham Organization plans to construct a 533,487-square-foot, three-building mixed-use development at 12040 Flatlands Avenue, 395 Louisiana Avenue and 12030 Flatlands Avenue in East New York. The buildings will stand between 12 and 14 stories tall and have a combined 599 housing units. Gloria Glas is the architect behind the project. Job Filing Numbers: B01412657-I1, B01412138-I1, B01412655-I1 📊 New Building: In Long Island City, ZD Jasper plans to build a 23-story, mixed-use building at 35-25 Steinway Street. It will measure 120,490 square feet and house 99 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gotham Organization files 533,487 SF, three-building mixed-use in East New York; 599 units, 12-14 stories.
ZD Jasper plans 23-story, 99-unit project at 35-25 Steinway Street, Long Island City (120,490 SF).
Mendy Schwimmer files 99-unit, nine-story project (105,210 SF) at 70 West Street, Greenpoint.
stories.summary: NYC's top construction permits filed in week ending June 12, 2026 included Gotham Organization's three-building, 599-unit, 533,487-square-foot mixed-use development in East New York (12-14 stories); ZD Jasper's 23-story, 99-apartment project at 35-25 Steinway Street in Long Island City (120,490 square feet); and Mendy Schwimmer's nine-story, 99-unit mixed-use project at 70 West Street in Greenpoint (105,210 square feet).
stories.impact: A steady pipeline of mid-rise multifamily permits in outer-borough submarkets (East New York, Long Island City, Greenpoint) reflects continued NYC multifamily development activity, driven by zoning flexibility, institutional debt availability (Gotham's $315M loan), and demographic demand. For developers, this signals: (1) competition intensifying in affordable/workforce-housing niches (Gotham), (2) sustained permit-filing velocity in Queens and Brooklyn despite constrained financing, and (3) concentration of new supply in transit-accessible, zoning-friendly corridors. Near-term deliveries will pressure rents and absorption timelines across these submarkets.
